posts.title: TODO — UX principles of this portfolio (rename me)
posts.summary: TODO — short summary, two sentences. Why a CV with sliders, why these four axes.

body.text: TODO — opening paragraph. Frame why this portfolio looks the way it does — one CV, four sliders, parameterised the way a writer parameterises tone. Reader should leave knowing the question I was answering.

TODO — explain density, polish, hierarchy, motion. Each axis is a real reading-experience trade-off, not a Material Design dial. Density picks how much text you want at once. Polish picks brutalist vs. refined. Hierarchy picks flat vs. dramatic. Motion picks static vs. kinetic.
TODO — pullquote: a memorable line that captures the principle.
What I'd never make a slider
TODO — the negative space. The CV content is fixed. Tone is a discrete toggle, not a slider. Match percentages on /jd are deliberately not numerical. Where I drew the line and why.

items: TODO — sliders that animate text size cause layout thrash; only colors/border/spacing transition.
TODO — kinetic mode at full bore is unreadable; the system caps motion before it harms comprehension.
TODO — refining polish past 0.85 collapses the dramatic hierarchy; the two axes aren't independent.

body.text: TODO — closing paragraph. The point isn't that the sliders are clever; it's that 'CV' is a multi-dimensional document and pretending it isn't ships an inferior CV.
